coredata.dc:title: Quaternary ammonium salt-derived carbon dots for antibacterial efficacy and tetracycline sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Siqi
Qi, Haiyan
Yi, Tonghui
Jing, Tao
Li, Jun
Gao, Yang
Zeng, Qingxin
Zhao, Hongxu
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections pose a significant threat to global health, necessitating the development of novel antibacterial materials. Tetracycline antibiotics (CTC, OTC, TC) are low-cost and widely abused in recent years. However, they were easily absorbed by the human body and long-term exposure to tetracycline could contribute to some adverse reactions and diseases. So there is a need for novel antimicrobial agents and a rapid, sensitive detection method of tetracycline antibiotics. In this study, we synthesized three carbon dots (CDs1, CDs2, CDs3) from quaternary ammonium salts via a hydrothermal method. Antibacterial assays against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli demonstrated their potent antibacterial activity with minimum inhibitory concentrations(MIC) determined as 39 μg/mL, 78 μg/mL, and 9.8 μg/mL, respectively. Notably, CDs3 exhibited the highest antibacterial effectiveness, attributed to its smaller size and higher chlorine content compared to CDs1 and CDs2. Additionally, CDs1, characterized by its high fluorescence quantum yield and stability, demonstrated remarkable specificity and robust anti-interference capabilities in detecting tetracycline antibiotics, including chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and tetracycline (TC). Leveraging the internal filtration effect mechanism, CDs1 exhibited detection limits of 0.18 μmol/L for CTC, 0.15 μmol/L for OTC, and 0.13 μmol/L for TC, respectively. The effectiveness of this detection method was further confirmed through validation in real milk samples, confirming the practical utility of CDs1 in food safety testing. The low toxicity of carbon dots were synthesized using a simple one-step hydrothermal method, which was fast, cost-effective, and easy to achieve large-scale production. Our findings underscore the potential of CDs derived from quaternary ammonium salts, demonstrating promising performance in antibacterial applications and providing insights into food safety testing methodologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Quaternary ammonium salt
Antibacterial activity
Tetracycline antibiotics
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of antibacterial activity of CDs
Detection of tetracycline antibiotics in milk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation and reaction mechanism of CDs1 synthesized from quaternary ammonium salts for detecting tetracycline antibiotics.
HRTEM of CDs1 (a), CDs2 (b), and CDs3 (c); Particle size distribution analysis of CDs1 (d), CDs2 (e), and CDs3 (f).
X-ray diffraction spectra(a) and FT-IR spectra(b) of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3.
C1 s XPS of CDs1 (a), CDs2 (d), and CDs3 (g); N1 s XPS of CDs1 (b), CDs2 (e), and CDs3 (h); O1 s XPS of CDs1 (c), CDs2 (f), and CDs3 (i).
Fluorescence spectra of CDs1 (a), CDs2 (b), and CDs3(c); normalized spectra of CDs1(d), CDs2 (e), and CDs3 (f); CIE chromaticity diagram of CDs1(g), CDs2 (h), and CDs3(i).
Growth curves of Escherichia coli (a, b, c) and Staphylococcus aureus (d, e, f) in response to CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 at 1/2 MIC, MIC, and 2MIC concentrations.
Cell survival rate after treatment with different concentrations of CDs1, CDs2 and CDs3.
Light stability (a), salt tolerance (b), and pH stability (c) of CDs1.
Fluorescence of mixed solutions of CDs1 and CTC (a), OTC (b), and TC (c)with or without interfering substances.
Fluorescence quenching curves of chlortetracycline (a), oxytetracycline (b), and tetracycline (c); Fluorescence fitting curves of chlortetracycline (d), oxytetracycline (e), and tetracycline (f).
(a) Fluorescence attenuation curves of CDs1 and CDs1and tetracycline antibiotics; (b) UV–vis spectra of tetracycline antibiotics and excitation and emission spectra of CDs1.
Minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 against
Performance comparison of different methods for detecting tetracycline antibiotics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery rates of spiked-in tetracycline antibiotics (CTC,OTC and TC) in pretreated milk samples.

head.ce:title: Quaternary ammonium salt-derived carbon dots for antibacterial efficacy and tetracycline sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cTechnology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation, Qiqihar, Heilongjiang 161006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections pose a significant threat to global health, necessitating the development of novel antibacterial materials. Tetracycline antibiotics (CTC, OTC, TC) are low-cost and widely abused in recent years. However, they were easily absorbed by the human body and long-term exposure to tetracycline could contribute to some adverse reactions and diseases. So there is a need for novel antimicrobial agents and a rapid, sensitive detection method of tetracycline antibiotics. In this study, we synthesized three carbon dots (CDs1, CDs2, CDs3) from quaternary ammonium salts via a hydrothermal method. Antibacterial assays against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli demonstrated their potent antibacterial activity with minimum inhibitory concentrations(MIC) determined as 39 μg/mL, 78 μg/mL, and 9.8 μg/mL, respectively. Notably, CDs3 exhibited the highest antibacterial effectiveness, attributed to its smaller size and higher chlorine content compared to CDs1 and CDs2. Additionally, CDs1, characterized by its high fluorescence quantum yield and stability, demonstrated remarkable specificity and robust anti-interference capabilities in detecting tetracycline antibiotics, including chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and tetracycline (TC). Leveraging the internal filtration effect mechanism, CDs1 exhibited detection limits of 0.18 μmol/L for CTC, 0.15 μmol/L for OTC, and 0.13 μmol/L for TC, respectively. The effectiveness of this detection method was further confirmed through validation in real milk samples, confirming the practical utility of CDs1 in food safety testing. The low toxicity of carbon dots were synthesized using a simple one-step hydrothermal method, which was fast, cost-effective, and easy to achieve large-scale production. Our findings underscore the potential of CDs derived from quaternary ammonium salts, demonstrating promising performance in antibacterial applications and providing insights into food safety testing methodologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections have become increasingly prevalent, representing a significant global health challenge [1,2]. The primary clinical strategy for managing these infections remains the use of antibiotics. However, the widespread and frequent use of antibiotics has facilitated the acquisition of resistance genes by pathogens through various mechanism [3]. This has resulted in the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, including multidrug-resistant and extensively drug-resistant strains, commonly referred to as “superbugs” [4,5]. These resistant strains have significantly complicated treatment strategies, underscoring the urgent need for novel antimicrobial agents with mechanisms distinct from traditional antibiotics [6].

Recent advancements in nanotechnology have introduced innovative paradigms for the development of antibacterial materials [7–9]. Nanomaterials, characterized by their diminutive size (typically <10 nm) [10], exhibit enhanced antibacterial properties attributable to their increased surface-area-to-volume ratio (SA:V) [11]. This nanoscale feature facilitates more effective interactions with bacterial cells, leading to improved antimicrobial efficacy. Various nanomaterials, including antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) [12,13], silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [14,15], and carbon dots (CDs) [16], have demonstrated promise in antimicrobial applications.

AMPs are short, cationic, and amphiphilic peptides exhibiting broad-spectrum antibacterial activity with reduced resistance development. Despite their promising antimicrobial properties, AMPs face significant challenges in biomedical applications, such as pronounced hemolytic activity and high production costs. AgNPs have demonstrated potent antibacterial activity, however, their cytotoxicity to mammalian cells remains a major limitation. In contrast, CDs exhibit low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and high safety. CDs enhance membrane permeability and function as efflux pump inhibitors, making them less likely to induce bacterial resistance compared to conventional antibiotics [17]. Consequently, the exploration of antibacterial CDs has emerged as a prominent area of research [18,19].

Beyond their antibacterial properties, CDs have found extensive applications in the field of bio sensing, attributed to their high quantum yield, exceptional optical stability, and robust chemical stability [20]. Recent research efforts have demonstrated the synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus, utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions [21]. Additionally, CDs derived from citric acid/β - alanine have shown effective antibacterial activity [22]. Literature reports have focused more on individual applications of CDs, such as fluorescence detection or antibacterial activity, but there were still few reports on the multifunctional applications of CDs. Moreover, some investigations emphasize the antibacterial properties of CDs, most of them use metal doping to achieve their goals [23,24]. Therefore, the synthesis of non-metallic doped carbon dots for both antibacterial and sensing applications is of crucial significance.

In this study, we synthesized three different types of CDs (CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3) using only quaternary ammonium salts (QAS) with varying alkyl chain lengths using a simple one-step hydrothermal method (Scheme 1). Antibacterial assays against Gram-positive strain (Staphylococcus aureus) and Gram-negative strain (Escherichia coli) demonstrated potent antibacterial activity, with CDs3 exhibiting the highest efficacy, attributed to its smaller size and higher chlorine content. Additionally, CDs1, characterized by its notable fluorescence quantum yield and stability, was examined for its potential in detecting tetracycline antibiotics, including CTC, OTC, and TC, utilizing the internal filtration effect. The feasibility of employing CDs1 for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics in real milk samples was demonstrated. Overall, this research contributes to the development of novel antibacterial materials and offers a new approach for tetracycline detection, addressing critical challenges in both healthcare and environmental monitoring (Scheme 1 ).

Cetylpyridinium Chloride (CPC), Cetrimonium Bromide (CTAB), dodecyl dimethyl ammonium chloride (DDAC), chlortetracycline (CTC), were bought from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Chloramphenicol was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glycine (Gly) was acquired from Sino pharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Calcium chloride was obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent No.1 Factory (Tianjin, China), and nickel sulphate from Tianjin Ke miou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was acquired from Bi yuntian Biotechnology Co., Ltd(Nanjing, China). Staphylococcus aureus (CICC10384) and E. coli(ML-35) were obtained from the China Center of Industrial Culture Collection(Beijing, China). Mueller Hinton (M-H) broth was acquired from High tech Industrial Park Hai bo Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Qingdao, China). DMEM-H high sugar culture medium purchased from Sigma Aldrich in the United States. The normal human hepatocyte cell line (HL-7702) was obtained from the Chinese Academy of Sciences Cell Bank (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Zhejiang Tianhang Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Zhejiang, China), and trypsin-EDTA solution was acquired from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments. Cell culture reagents were handled under sterile conditions to prevent contamination, and all experiments involving bacterial cultures were conducted in a biosafety cabinet to ensure aseptic conditions.

Three variants of CDs (CDs1, CDs2, CDs3) were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using different quaternary ammonium salts: CTAB for CDs1, CPC for CDs2, and DDAC for CDs3. Initially, 0.1 mol of each of the aforementioned salts (CTAB, CPC, and DDAC) was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water with ultrasonic assistance until complete dissolution. The solutions were then transferred to an autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 6 h. After coolinga period of time, the reaction mixtures underwent dialysis using membranes with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 500 Da for 24 h. Subsequently, the mixtures were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and freeze-dried to obtain solid CDs powders. The synthesized CDs, denoted as CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3, were then stored under 4 °C for subsequent characterization.

The morphological and structural characteristics of CDs were assessed using a JEOL JEM-2100Ffield emission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis spectroscopy were investigated using a Pu xi TU-1901 UV–VIS spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). Excitation and emission spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). The functional groups present in CDs were identified using a Thermo Fisher Nicolet 380 FT-IR spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA) spanning the spectral range of 650 to 4000 cm-1. The crystallographic properties of CDs were analyzed using a Bruker D8-FOCUS X-ray diffraction system (Billerica, MA, USA). Surface chemical structure analysis was performed using a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer microprobe(Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using an Edinburgh FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Livingston, UK). Bacterial cultures were incubated in a BILONCOS-200B benchtop full temperature shaking incubator (Shanghai, China), while antibacterial assays were conducted within a Shuli vertical laminar flow cabinet SW-CJ-2D (Shanghai, China).

To evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of CDs, standardized bacterial cultures of Staphylococcus aureus(CICC10384) and E. coli(ML35) were prepared. Initially, 10 mL of MH culture medium was inoculated into sterilized Erlenmeyer flasks, followed by overnight incubation at 37 °C. Subsequently, the optical density (OD600) of the cultures was recorded using a spectrophotometer at 600 nm, allowing for the determination of colony concentrations. The bacterial suspension was diluted to a final concentration of 2 × 107colony-forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL).

For the antibacterial activity assays, a 96-well plate was utilized. Each well was loaded with 70 μL of the diluted bacterial suspension and 70 μL of CDs solutions at varying concentrations: 2500 μg/mL, 1250 μg/mL, 625 μg/mL, 312.5 μg/mL, 156.2 μg/mL, 78.1 μg/mL, 39 μg/mL, 19.5 μg/mL, and 9.8 μg/mL. The negative control group consisted of 70 μL of MH broth, while the positive control contained 70 μL of 1.12 mg/mL polymyxin, a known antibiotic. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was determined through turbidity observation after overnight incubation. Additionally, to assess the dynamic antibacterial activity over time, diluted bacterial suspensions were exposed to CDs at concentrations corresponding to MIC, 1/2MIC, and 2 MIC over 24 h, with control groups undergoing incubation under similar conditions with medium alone. Samples were collected at various time, and the optical density (OD600) were recorded to generate kinetic curves for analysis.

The normal human hepatocyte HL-7702 cells were cultivated in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) high-glucose medium supplemented with 10 % FBS at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. Cells in the logarithmic growth phase were digested with trypsin-EDTA solution and seeded at a density of 50,000 cells per well in 96-well plates. After 12 h, the culture medium was replaced with 100 μL of CDs solution at various concentrations. Each concentration was tested in quadruplicate. The control group received 100 μL of DMEM high-glucose medium without CDs solution. After 24 h of exposure to CDs, cell viability was assessed using the CCK-8 assay. Briefly, the culture medium was substituted for 100 μL of CCK-8 solution diluted at a 10:1 ratio and we incubate the cells for another 2 h. Subsequently, the absorbance at 450 nm (OD450) was measured using a microplate reader.

Quinine sulfate was used as a reference standard with a known quantum yield (
Y s
) of 0.54.The fluorescence emission peak area and UV absorbance of CDs were measured at 350 nm. Subsequently, the relative fluorescence quantum yield (
Y x
) was computed by the following formula:
Y x
=
Y s
×
F x
F s
×
A s
A x
×
(
η s
η x
)
2
Where Y is the fluorescence quantum yield, A is the absorbance, F is the fluorescence integral area, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and s and x refer to the reference and the sample, respectively.

To facilitate the effective detection of tetracycline antibiotics, CDs1, characterized by its high fluorescence quantum yield and stability, was selected as the optimal candidate for analytical detection. Briefly, solutions of three distinct tetracycline antibiotics—CTC, OTC, and TC—were meticulously prepared at concentrations of 10 mM. Subsequently, a quartz cuvette was employed, into which 2 mL of deionized water, 400 μL of CDs1 solution, and 30 μL of varied concentrations of the aforementioned tetracycline solutions were added. The resulting mixture was thoroughly mixed, and the emission spectra were measured (λex=350 nm).

To assess tetracycline antibiotics within authentic samples, bulk milk sample sourced from a commercial vendor was utilized. The sample underwent a pre-treatment procedure as described previously [25]. Subsequently, the treated milk specimen was added into CDs1 solution, and its emission spectrum was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Utilizing a pre-defined standard curve, the resulting spectral data was analyzed to determine the precise concentration of tetracycline antibiotics within the milk sample.

Three types of CDs (CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3) were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method, each employing a distinct quaternary ammonium salt: CTAB for CDs1, CPC for CDs2, and DDAC for CDs3. HRTEM analysis revealed the morphological characteristics of the three CDs, demonstrating their well-dispersed and predominantly spherical nature (Fig. 1 a-c). The average particle sizes of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 were determined to be 3.08 nm, 2.78 nm, and 2.09 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d-f). The insets showed that the three CDs had no graphitic structure. Furthermore, XRD patterns exhibited distinctive absorption peak at 23.63 °, 20.18 °, and 22.83 °, indicating their amorphous carbon structure (Fig. 2 a) [26].

Next, FT-IR was employed to elucidate the functional groups present on the surface of CDs (Fig. 2b). Notably, common absorption peaks were observed among all three CDs. Specifically, the absorption peak at 3400 cm-1corresponded to the NH stretching vibration [27,28], while peaks at 2922 cm-1 and 2850 cm-1were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CH bond in CH3 and CH2 groups, respectively [29]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1718 cm−1 were attributed to the C = O stretching vibration [30]. The peaks observed at 1640 cm-1, were assigned to the C = C stretching vibrations [31]. Additionally, characteristic peaks at1462 cm-1, 1481 cm-1, and 1470 cm-1 were associated with the CH bending vibrations, while the absorption peak at1380cm-1corresponded to the CN stretching vibration [32]. Furthermore, the peak observed at 1162 cm-1indicated the presence of CO bonds. Overall, the structural analysis revealed a high degree of similarity among the functional groups present in all three CDs, contributing to their favorable water solubility and potential for diverse applications.

XPS was employed for a detailed analysis of the chemical composition of CDs. The full XPS spectra of CDs indicated the presence of C, O, N, Cl, and Br elements, with distinct absorption peaks for C, N, and O observed at 284.43 eV, 401.38 eV, and 530.09 eV, respectively (Fig. S1). Notably, CDs1 and CDs3 exhibited additional absorption peaks corresponding to Cl at 197.36 eV, while CDs2 displayed adistinct absorption peak attributed to Br at 178.35 eV Quantitative analysis revealed the C content in CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 to be 87.27 %, 86.30 %, and 90.12 %, respectively. Additionally, the Cl content in CDs1 and CDs3 was determined to be 2.43 % and 2.90 %, respectively. High-resolution C1 s XPS analysis revealed absorption peaks at 283.75 eV and 284.82 eV, owing to C = C/CC and CO/CN bonds, respectively [33,34] (Fig. 3 a, d, and g). The High-resolution N1 s spectra displayed an absorption peak near 401.4 eV, attributed to quaternary ammonium nitrogen [35,36] (Fig. 3b, e, and h). This indicated that CDs retained the structural characteristics of the raw material, further demonstrating their potential in the field of antibacterial activity. The O1 s spectra exhibited an absorption peak at 531.07 eV corresponding to CO bonding (Fig. 3c, f, and i). Notably, the O1 s spectrum of CDs3 also exhibited an additional absorption peak at 528.78 eV, indicative of OH bonding [37,38], that might be the key reason for the optimal antibacterial effect (Fig. 3i). The Cl 2p spectra of CDs1 and CDs3 further demonstrated the presence of Cl with two absorption bands at 196.9 eV and 198.5 eV, corresponding to CCl and NCl bonds, respectively (Fig. S2). These XPS findings aligned closely with the FT-IR data (Fig. 2b), providing comprehensive insights into the chemical composition and surface functionalities of CDs.

The UV spectra of CDs exhibited prominent absorption peaks at 288 nm, attributed to the B absorption band resulting from π→π* transitions, indicative of the presence of C = C bonds. This suggested that CDs retained some structural characteristics of quaternary ammonium salt [39] (Fig. S3).

Visually, the solutions of all three CDs appeared pale yellow under natural light and emitted a bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S3 inset). This fluorescence behavior was further investigated by determining the optimal excitation and corresponding emission wavelengths. The optimal excitation wavelengths of CDs1, CDs2, and CDs3 were determined to be 350 nm, 320 nm, and 330 nm, respectively, with corresponding emission wavelengths of 430 nm, 420 nm, and 412 nm (Fig. 4 a-c).To facilitate a comparative analysis, the emission spectra were normalized (Fig. 4d-f), revealing shifts in the emission peak positions of CDs corresponding to variations in the excitation wavelength. Notably, the emission peaks displayed a redshift with increasing excitation wavelength, thus highlighting the excitation wavelength dependence of CDs.

In addition to spectral analysis, the color coordinates of CDs were computed using the CIE color space, resulting in coordinates of (0.1532, 0.1131) for CDs1, (0.1575, 0.1368) for CDs2, and (0.1539, 0.1333) for CDs3. These coordinates corresponded to specific regions within the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4g-i), consistent with the observed fluorescence colors under UV light (Fig, S3). This alignment between calculated color coordinates and visual observations offered a comprehensive understanding of the optical properties of CDs. Collectively, the UV-absorption spectra, fluorescence emission behavior, and colorimetric data provided valuable insights into the optical characteristics and fluorescence behavior of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antibacterial activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Variations in carbon nanomaterial size play a pivotal role in determining their antibacterial performance [40]. Typically, smaller particle sizes are associated with enhanced antibacterial activity due to increased cellular uptake and DNA interaction [41]. Thus, the superior antibacterial efficacy of CDs3 compared to CDs1 and CDs2 might be attributed to its smaller granule size. Additionally, the presence Cl-doped CDs, such as CDs1 and CDs3, exhibited enhanced antibacterial effects relative to Br-doped CDs, like CDs2. Consequently, the combination of a smaller particle size and higher Cl content in CDs3 contributed to its exceptional antibacterial efficacy, highlighting the importance of these factors in optimizing the antibacterial properties of CDs for potential biomedical applications.

The antibacterial properties of three CDs have been determined. Then, the cytotoxicity of the three CDs was implemented. As shown in Fig. 6 , when the concentration of three CDs were increased to 800 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was as high as 89.02 %, indicating that all CDs had low toxicity and excellent application in the field of antibacterial [42].

The optimization of the three CDs’ preparation conditions were assessed by fluorescence quantum yield, and the reaction time and temperature were investigated (Table S1-S3). Among the 27 groups of prepared carbon dots, the highest quantum yield of CDs1(37.76 %) was achieved when the reaction conditions were set at a temperature of 190 °C, a reaction time of 6 h, making it the best candidate for subsequent applications.

To evaluate the stability of CDs1, a comprehensive analysis was conducted under varied conditions, including prolonged illumination, ion intensity, and pH fluctuations. Remarkably, under extended UV lamp irradiation lasting up to 120 min, the fluorescence intensity of CDs1 exhibited only a slight decrease, indicative of exceptional photo stability and satisfactory resistance to photo bleaching (Fig. 7 a) [43]. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CDs1 demonstrated minimal alteration with increasing NaCl concentration from 0.2 mM to 1.0 mM, signifying robust resistance against salt-induced fluorescence quenching (Fig. 7b). Given the critical role of pH in practical detection scenarios, the fluorescence intensity of CDs1 was assessed across a pH spectrum. Noteworthy, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a gradual increment from pH 1 to 7, reaching its peak at pH 7, before exhibiting a swift decline with in the pH range of 8 to 14 (Fig. 7c). This observation underscored the broad pH tolerance of CDs1, making it suitable for utilization in environments characterized by acidic or weakly alkaline conditions.

To elucidate the interaction mechanism between CDs1 and tetracycline antibiotics, fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted (Fig. 10 a). The average fluorescence lifetime (τ average) of CDs1 was determined to be 2.73 ns. Upon the introduction of CTC, OTC, and TC, the τ average values were calculated as 2.34 ns, 2.55 ns, and 2.54 ns, respectively. Remarkably, the fluorescence decay curves of CDs1 exhibited minimal alteration upon the introduction of tetracycline antibiotics, suggesting a static quenching [51] or internal filtration effect (IFE) [52] quenching mechanism. Further investigations were undertaken to elucidate the quenching mechanism of CDs1 (Fig. S5). Notably, no emergence of new peaks was discernible in the ultraviolet spectrum of CDs1 following the addition of tetracycline antibiotics, and the position of the absorption peak of CDs1 remained unaltered. This observation negated the occurrence of static quenching, thus ruling it out as the predominant quenching mechanism. Moreover, the UV spectrum of CDs1 revealed an absorption peak at 300 nm, exhibiting significant overlap with the excitation spectrum of CDs1 (Fig. 10b). Consequently, it was inferred that the interaction mechanism between CDs1 and tetracycline antibiotics was owing to the internal filtration effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline antibiotics in milk
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability and accuracy of CDs1 in detecting tetracycline antibiotics, a bulk milk sample obtained from the market was selected. Following pretreatment as described previously [21], the tetracycline antibiotic content was analyzed in these prepared milk samples. Remarkably, it revealed no detectable traces of tetracycline antibiotics across the tested milk samples (Table 3 ). Subsequently, to evaluate the recovery efficiency of CDs1, three distinct concentrations of tetracycline antibiotic solutions(5 μmol/L, 50 μmol/L, and 110 μmol/L) were spiked into the milk samples, followed by analysis. The results illustrated recoveries ranging from 96.4 % to 107.6 % (RSD<2.35 %). These findings underscored the high practicality and precision of CDs1 in analyzing real-world samples.

In this study, three distinct CDs (CDs1, CDs2, CDs3) were synthesized utilizing three different quaternary ammonium salts (CTAB, CPC, and DDAC) as precursor materials through a hydrothermal synthesis method conducted at 190 °C for 6 h. Detailed characterization analyses unveiled revealed their exceptional water solubility and revealed particle sizes of 2.78 nm for CDs1, 3.08 nm for CDs2, and 2.09 nm for CDs3. Antibacterial evaluations were performed utilizing Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli as model organisms. Remarkably, all CDs exhibited potent antibacterial efficacy, with MIC scalculated as 39 μg/mL for CDs1, 78 μg/mL for CDs2, and 9.8 μg/mL for CDs3. Notably, the superior antibacterial activity of CDs3 was attributed to its smaller particle size and higher Cl element content. Moreover, CDs1, distinguished by its high fluorescence quantum yield and stability, demonstrated effectiveness in detecting tetracycline antibiotics, a capability successfully translated to the analysis of tetracycline antibiotics in real milk samples, showcasing its practical utility in food safety assessments. In summary, these three CDs showcase remarkable antibacterial properties and hold promise in enhancing food safety through reliable detection methodologies.

Siqi Shen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Haiyan Qi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tonghui Yi: Data curation. Tao Jing: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Jun Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yang Gao: Software. Qingxin Zeng: Software. Hongxu Zhao: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172092); the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (145309525); the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (GJQSCYH-2022001).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.140620.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tackling antibiotic resistance: the environmental framework
Nat. Rev. Microbiol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Berendonk, T.U., Manaia, C.M., Merlin, C., et al., Tackling antibiotic resistance: the environmental framework. Nat. Rev. Microbiol., 2015, 13(5):310–7. DOI:10.1038/nrmicro3439.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bacterial co-infection and secondary infection in patients with COVID-19: a living rapid review and meta-analysis
Clin. Microbiol. Infect.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Langford J.B., So M., Raybardhan S., et al. Bacterial co-infection and secondary infection in patients with COVID-19: a living rapid review and meta-analysis. Clin. Microbiol. Infect., 2020, 26(12):1622–1629. DOI: 10.1016/j.cmi.2020.07.016
